_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: File Handling practice 1
questions.prompt: Which mode adds new text without deleting existing text?
questions.explanation: Append mode `a` positions writes at the end. Write mode `w` replaces existing contents, while read mode does not permit writing.

questions.title: File Handling practice 2
choices: To remove the newline around each line
To close the file
To convert each line to a number
To skip the first line
questions.explanation: File iteration keeps the newline character at the end of most lines. `strip()` removes it so `print` does not add an extra blank line.

questions.title: Trace File Handling
questions.code:
with open("courses.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    file.write("CS101\n")
    file.write("MATH120\n")

with open("courses.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    print(file.read())
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The `with` block owns the open file. When the indented block ends, Python closes it even if an error interrupted the work. The resulting output is 'CS101\nMATH120'.

questions.title: File Handling good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File Handling?
choices: Use `with open(...)` to guarantee closing.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `with open(...)` to guarantee closing. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File Handling practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    text = file.read()
print(text)

with open("notes.txt") as file:
    pass
text = file.read()
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Keeping the read inside `with` avoids trying to use a closed file later. The text itself can be saved and used after the block.

questions.title: File Handling practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Writing can replace real data'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Check the mode before running file code. Opening an existing file with `"w"` clears it immediately. Choose `"a"` when the goal is to preserve the old text and add another record.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Check the mode before running file code. Opening an existing file with `"w"` clears it immediately. Choose `"a"` when the goal is to preserve the old text and add another record.

questions.title: File Handling practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File Handling?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Specify UTF-8 for text you control.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Specify UTF-8 for text you control. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File Handling practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File Handling?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Confirm whether the mode reads, replaces, or appends.
questions.explanation: Confirm whether the mode reads, replaces, or appends. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File Handling practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File Handling?
choices: Expect missing paths and permission errors.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Expect missing paths and permission errors. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File Handling practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File Handling?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Process large files one line at a time.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Process large files one line at a time. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
